coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of carbon dots from cordyceps militaris and versatile applications in alcohol detection and waterproof fluorescent ink
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Jing
Zhou, Hongyu
Ma, Yanshun
Wu, Shaogui
Hao, Liang
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of highly efficient and low-toxicity fluorescent nanomaterials. In this study, using cordyceps militaris as a carbon source, we prepared green-emitting CDs with excellent properties, such as good photobleaching resistance, salt resistance and chemical stability, as well as high quantum yield. Further exploration of the versatile applications of G-CDs in chemical detection and information anti-counterfeiting fields revealed that G-CDs can not only be used as detection agent for analyzing alcohol content in alcoholic beverages but also serve as waterproof fluorescent ink for painting and writing due to their water-resistant property. This work is based on natural biomass as the main precursor to prepare fluorescent CDs with excellent luminescent characteristics and stable physicochemical properties. It is significant for reducing reliance on chemical reagents, lowering the cost of synthesizing CDs, and expanding the application range of CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cordyceps militaris
Fluorescent ink
Alcohol detection
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Estimation of relative fluorescence quantum yield
Detection of water content in ethanol

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Thank Prof. Dongmao Zhang, Ph.D Joanna Xiuzhu Xu, Xue Leng et al. in Department of Chemistry, Mississippi State University for helpful advices.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of green carbon dot synthesis.

Comparison of fluorescence spectra (a) and fluorescence intensity (b) of CDs synthesized by different formulations. (c-d) Comparison of fluorescence stability between G-CDs and R-CDs. Herein, the legends CM-CDs, OPD-CDs were prepared by solo cordyceps militaris extract and solo OPD solution respectively; G-CDs and R-CDs were synthesized by the mixture of cordyceps militaris extract and OPD solution.
Structural characterization. (a) Infrared spectrum of G-CDs. (b) XPS survey spectrum of G-CDs. (c–e) high resolution C1s, O1s and N1s of G-CDs, respectively.

Optical properties of G-CDs. (a) Three-dimensional excitation and emission spectrum, (b) Two-dimensional projection, (c) UV–Vis absorption spectrum (purple), excitation spectrum (blue), emission spectrum (multi-color). (d) Emission spectra of G-CDs at different excitation wavelengths. (e) Normalized Emission spectra.

Optical behavior of G-CDs. (a) Fluorescence spectra and (b) Fluorescence intensity of G-CDs in different solvents. (c) Integral fluorescence intensity versus absorbance in different solvents. (d) Comparison of quantum yields of G-CDs in different solvents. (e) Fluorescence intensity curves under different ultraviolet lamp irradiation time and (f) Fluorescence intensity changes with time.

Carbon dot solution serving as a detection agent for ethanol concentration in wine. (a) Water content dependent the fluorescence spectra of G-CDs. (b) The linear fitting of the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs in ethanol solution with water content. (c) The fluorecence spectra from five parallel experiments and (d) their detected alcohol contents.

CDs solution used as fluorescent anti-counterfeiting ink. (a-b) Both handwritings are invisible under sunlight, (c-d) visible under ultraviolet light, (e-f) under ultraviolet light after washing with tap water. Note: Panes a, c, e are the same handwriting by G-Dots; Panes b, d, f are the handwriting by B-CDs for comparison, which are prepared by citric acid and ethylenediamine.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of carbon dots from cordyceps militaris and versatile applications in alcohol detection and waterproof fluorescent ink

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu, 610066, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu 610066, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Tsinghua University, Beijing, 100084, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of highly efficient and low-toxicity fluorescent nanomaterials. In this study, using cordyceps militaris as a carbon source, we prepared green-emitting CDs with excellent properties, such as good photobleaching resistance, salt resistance and chemical stability, as well as high quantum yield. Further exploration of the versatile applications of G-CDs in chemical detection and information anti-counterfeiting fields revealed that G-CDs can not only be used as detection agent for analyzing alcohol content in alcoholic beverages but also serve as waterproof fluorescent ink for painting and writing due to their water-resistant property. This work is based on natural biomass as the main precursor to prepare fluorescent CDs with excellent luminescent characteristics and stable physicochemical properties. It is significant for reducing reliance on chemical reagents, lowering the cost of synthesizing CDs, and expanding the application range of CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of fluorescent nanomaterials with excellent luminescent properties, photostability, low cytotoxicity, significant water solubility, good biocompatibility, low environmental risk, and significant surface modification flexibility [1,2]. As a versatile fluorescent nanomaterial, CDs have shown wide application prospects in the detection of anions [3–5], metal ions [6,7], pH [8,9], small biomolecules [10,11] and environmental pollutants [12,13]. The surface structure of CDs is rich in functional groups such as amino groups, carboxyl groups and hydroxyl groups [14,15]. This enables them to target with the analyte through charge or energy transfer, hydrogen bonding or inner filter effect. This induces changes in the electronic structure on the surface of CDs which can be monitored by changes in fluorescence signals to achieve determination of target substances. For example, Lin et al. successfully constructed a nitrogen-doped red-emitting carbon dot fluorescence sensing platform for sensitive detection of Fe3+ using amino salicylic acid as a precursor [16]. Xiao et al. prepared dual-emission fluorescent CDs and successfully achieved synchronous detection of lysine and pH by utilizing changes in emission peak ratio at 440 nm and 624 nm respectively. The established ratiometric detection method effectively overcomes background interference and significantly improves sensitivity and accuracy of detection [17].

On the other hand, the development of anti-counterfeiting ink technology is also attracting the interest of many researchers currently. For example, Kashmitha Muthamma et al. [18] introduced the latest progress in preparing anti-counterfeiting ink using various carbon-based nanomaterials such as carbon nanodots, carbon quantum dots, graphene quantum dots and carbogenic polymer dots to achieve advanced anti-counterfeiting technology. In addition, Namratha Ulla et al. [19] revealed that CDs synthesized from cassava exhibit unique optical properties in environmentally friendly ink formulations. However, currently most fluorescent carbon dot-based anti-counterfeiting inks have water solubility and their fluorescence intensity will be significantly quenched when exposed to water due to insufficient stability. Therefore, it is crucial to prepare fluorescent inks that are both waterproof and counterfeit-proof for enhancing the effectiveness of anti-counterfeiting measures while avoiding interference from external environments.

Meanwhile, in order to pursue excellent optical properties, a large amount of chemical reagents are generally used in the synthesis of CDs, which increases toxicity and seriously threatens the natural environment and human health [20,21]. Therefore, using environmentally friendly raw materials to synthesize CDs has become a trend [22–24]. Recently, Namratha Ullal et al. explored different types of green raw materials as sources for synthesizing undoped and doped CDs and summarized the significant advantages of obtaining CDs from environmentally friendly precursors through green methods [25]. However, there are also some limitations, such as the need to further improve the stability of synthesized fluorescent CDs. Therefore, how to improve the optical properties of fluorescent CDs is also one of the contents that needs to be explored in this work.

This study used environmentally friendly raw materials (cordyceps militariss) as carbon sources, green-emitive CDs (G-CDs) with good photobleaching resistance, salt resistance, and chemical stability were successfully prepared. The research shows that the emission wavelength of the CDs is around 537 nm, and the quantum yield is as high as 12.9%. It was found that water has a significant quenching effect on the fluorescence of G-CDs. Therefore, this study successfully used G-CDs as a detection agent for analyzing alcohol content in alcoholic beverages. Further exploration of the multifunctional uses of fluorescent CDs in chemical detection and information anti-counterfeiting revealed that they can also be used as waterproof fluorescent ink for painting, writing, and anti-counterfeiting purposes. These applications are of great significance for for expanding the application of CDs synthesized from natural products.

Cordyceps militaris was purchased from Chengdu vegetable market. O-phenylenediamine (OPD, analytical grade) was obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Thiourea, HNO3 and ethanol (analytical grade) were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Co., Ltd. Citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) were purchased from Tianjin Comiou Chemical reagent Co., Ltd. The water used in the synthesis was secondary distilled water.

The hydrothermal reaction vessel (25 mL) was purchased from Xiniu Technology Co., Ltd. The ultrasonic cleaning machine was purchased from Shanghai Fuyang Instrument Co., Ltd. The electric constant temperature blast drying oven was purchased from Shanghai Lichen Scientific and Technological Development Co., Ltd. All UV–vis spectra were measured by the Shunyu Hengping 756 PC UV–vis spectrophotometer, and the fluorescence spectra were obtained by the Lengguang F98 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

Herein, cordyceps militaris as carbon source with OPD as nitrogen source, G-CDs were synthesized using a solvothermal method with ethanol as solvent. The detailed operation is as follows: first, 10 g of cordyceps militaris (CM) was weighed and added to 200 mL of ethanol. The mixture was then ground in a food processor, soaked for 12 h, and filtered. Next, 10 mL of the cordyceps militaris extract was mixed with 1.2 g of OPD, followed by stirring and ultrasonicating for 10 min. The resulting solution was transferred to a high-pressure reaction kettle and reacted at 180 °C for 6 h. After natural cooling to room temperature and purification by dialysis, G-CDs were obtained. The synthesis process is illustrated in Fig. 1 . For comparison, cordyceps militaris and OPD were separately used as precursors to synthesize CDs. For reference they were named as CM-CDs and OPD-CDs,respectively.

To compare the waterproof properties of G-CDs, another classic blue CDs (B-CDs) were synthesized by citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) [26]: 2 mmol CA and 8 mmol EDA are dissolved in 10 mL secondary distilled water and ultrasonicated for 30 min. Then the mixture is loaded into a 25 mL polytetrafluoroethylene lined hydrothermal reactor and heated for 5 h at 170 °C. After that, the reactor is cooled naturally. Then the B-CDs solution was obtained through dialysis purification.

Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR): The purified carbon dot solution was placed in an oven and thoroughly dried at 60 °C. Carbon dot powder was mixed with KBr at a ratio of 1:100, ground, pressed into pellets, and then subjected to infrared testing with a scanning range of 4000–400 cm−1.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS): The dried CDs powder was tested for surface composition using a Geneis 60s X-ray photoelectron spectrometer, with C1s (284.6 eV) as the internal standard for binding energy correction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of relative fluorescence quantum yield
ce:section.ce:para:
According to literature [27], the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M sulfuric acid solution is 0.54 at 360 nm. Using quinine sulfate as a standard reference, the relative QY is calculated by the following formula:
(1)
φ U
=
φ S
⋅
F U
F S
⋅
A S
A U
⋅
(
η S
η U
)
2
where φ represents the quantum yield; F represents the integrated fluorescence intensity; A represents absorbance at excitation wavelength; k represents the slope of fitting curve between integrated fluorescence intensity and absorbance; η represents refractive index of solvent; subscripts S and U refer to standard sample and test sample respectively. It should be noted that in order to avoid inner filter effect on quantum yield, absorbance (A) of both standard and test samples should be less than 0.05. At low concentrations (A<0.05), integrated fluorescence intensity (F) not only depends on quantum yield but also concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of water content in ethanol
ce:section.ce:para: Ethanol solutions with different volume ratios of water (0%, 2%, 4%, 6%, 8% and 10%) were prepared. 1 mL of G-CDs solution with ethanol as solvent was added to each of the above solution and fully mixed, and the samples were measured to collect spectral data.

Diluted carbon dot solution with ethanol as solvent can be used as fluorescent ink for anti-counterfeiting purposes, suitable for use with fountain pens, seals, and inkjet printers to write or print on white paper. The handwritting is photographed under sunlight and UV light respectively, then rinsed with tap water before being slightly dried and photographed again under UV light.

In order to investigate the influence of raw materials, cordyceps militaris extract, OPD solution, and their mixture were used as precursors for synthesizing CDs respectively. The CDs synthesized from cordyceps militaris extract and OPD solution were named CM-CDs and OPD-CDs respectively, with emission wavelengths of 366 nm and 537 nm (Fig. 2 a); the product solution synthesized from the mixture contained green and red CDs with emission wavelengths of 537 nm and 599 nm respectively, named G-CDs and R-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2b, the fluorescence intensities of CM-CDs and OPD-CDs were low while G-CDs and R-CDs display enhanced fluorescence intensities. This indicates that the combination of CM as carbon source with OPD as nitrogen source can synthesize efficient CDs. Furthermore, the stability of G-CDs and R-CDs was further examined. As shown in Fig. 2c and d, over time the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs slightly increased while that of R-CDs sharply decreased, indicating that R-CDs are extremely unstable and easily degraded. On the other hand, the emission wavelength (537 nm) of G-CDs is close to the excitation wavelength (535 nm) of R-CDs. It is obvious that the existence of R-CDs may absorb the fluorescence emitted by G-CDs. Then, as R-CDs degrades, it weakens the absorption on fluorescence emitted by G-CDs resulting in a slight increase in fluorescence intensity for G-CDs. Therefore, the G-CDs were chosen as the final research object.

The surface functional groups of CDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), as shown in Fig. 3
. The peak at 3700-3600 cm−1 corresponds to the O–H/N–H stretching vibration absorption peak, while the peak at 3300-3100 cm−1 corresponds to the C–H stretching vibration absorption peak. The peak at 2353 cm−1 is attributed to the OCO stretching vibration absorption, and the peak at 1550 cm−1 corresponds to NO2 stretching vibration absorption. The peak at 1046 cm−1 is attributed to SO stretching vibration absorption, and the peak at 665 cm−1 attributed to S–C stretching vibration absorption. These results indicate that there are functional groups such as –NH2, –CN and –NO2 in CDs, besides possibly containing other functional groups like –OH. It can be seen that N and S elements have been successfully doped into the G-CDs.

The XPS characterization of the CDs is shown in Fig. 3b. In the full scan spectrum, the characteristic peaks of O1s, N1s and C1s are located at 534.1, 399.5 and 285.1 ev respectively, indicating that the CDs mainly consist of three elements: C, N and O. The atomic ratios of C, N and O are 76.47%, 17.24% and 6.29%, respectively. By decomposing the characteristic peak of C1s, four peaks at 291.5, 286.6, 285.8 and 284.8 ev were obtained (Fig. 3c), corresponding to COOH, C–O, C–N and C–C/CC functional groups respectively.The peak at O1s with a binding energy of 534.1 ev (Fig. 3d) is caused by the O–C bond while two decomposition peaks for N1s appear at binding energies of 400.6 and 399.2 ev (Fig. 3e), indicating that nitrogen exists mainly in forms such as amino nitrogen and pyridine nitrogen on the surface of CDs.Combined with infrared spectroscopy analysis results,it can be concluded that there exist functional groups such as –OH,-COOH,-NH2 on the surface of CDs.

We characterized the synthesized G-CDs using UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, excitation spectroscopy and emission spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 4a, G-CDs have only one excitation-emission peak. The corresponding two-dimensional projection is shown in Fig. 4b. It can be seen that the excitation region of G-CDs is mainly located at 400–500 nm and the emission region is mainly located at 500–600 nm. Fig. 4c shows the UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of G-CDs. The strong absorption band below 300 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum is a typical aromatic benzene ring absorption peak from π→π* electron transition of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms; In addition, there is an absorbance peak near 450 nm (excitation wavelength), which is attributed to n→π* electron transition from functional groups on the surface of the G-CDs [28,29]. Since this part of absorbed photons in fluorescence emission process, these functional groups are likely to be part of luminescent centers or fluorephores on CDs. According to the excitation and emission spectra analysis, G-CDs have maximum excitation and emission peaks located at around 450 nm and 537 nm, respectively. The space under emission spectrum is filled with color corresponding to its respective emitting wavelength. It can be seen that the G-CDs emit typical green light. In addition, the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of G-CDs emission spectrum is 78 nm, suggesting that the G-CDs have a narrow emission.

Furthermore, we also studied the luminescence behavior of G-CDs at different excitation wavelengths by gradually changing them within 410–460 nm. The corresponding emission spectra are displayed in Fig. 4d. Under different excitation wavelengths, these emission spectra are relatively close. If these emission spectra are normalized, as shown in Fig. 4e, it can be clearly seen that the emission wavelengths almost overlap, suggesting that the G-CDs show excitation-independent emissions. This result implies that the G-CDs might belong to molecular state CDs. In other words, the surface of G-CDs has a uniform chemical state, which is almost consistent with the defect type associated with photoluminescence behavior.

Next, we examined the photo-stability of G-CDs in different chemical environments. Several common solvents in the laboratory were selected: water (WAT), methanol (MET), ethanol (ETH), acetone (ACE), tetrahydrofuran (THF) and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF). The fluorescence spectra of G-CDs of the same concentration in these solvents were measured, and their maximum emission intensities were statistically analyzed for comparison. As shown in Fig. 5 a and b, G-CDs had the highest fluorescence intensity in DMF while lowest in water. This indicates that DMF and water are good and poor solvents for G-CDs, respectively. To further quantitatively measure the influence of solvents on the luminescent properties of G-CDs, the quantum yield of CDs in different solvents was measured. The integrated fluorescence intensity was plotted against absorbance, as shown in Fig. 5c. Then one can esimate the QYs of G-CDs in different solvents using Eq. (1). As shown in Fig. 5d, G-CDs have the highest QY in acetone with a value of 12.9%. Therefore it can be concluded that currently synthesized G-CDs have considerable luminescence ability which exceeds many other types of CDs' efficiency. We further tested the photobleaching resistance of G-CDs in ethanol by continuous irradiation under an ultraviolet lamp for 2 h. As shown in Fig. 5e and f, the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs not only did not decrease, but showed a slight enhancement, indicating that the G-CDs have good resistance to photobleaching and are conducive to long-term storage and use.

Through chemical stability analysis, it was found that water has a significant quenching effect on the fluorescence of G-CDs. Therefore, we attempted to use G-CDs for the analysis and detection of water content in alcoholic beverages. We prepared ethanol solutions with different water content (0%, 2%, 4%, 6%, 8% and 10%). The same volume of G-CDs solution was added to each solution and thoroughly mixed, and spectral data were collected from the samples. As shown in Fig. 6 a, as the water content increased, the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs decreased. In addition, the linear fitting curve between water content and G-CDs fluorescence intensity is as shown in Fig. 6b. Within a range of 2%–10% water content in ethanol solutions, there is a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs and water content with a linear correlation coefficient reaching up to 99.28%.

Then, we conducted actual detection on the white wine (labeled with an alcohol percentage by volume (vol) at 56%) sold in the market. As shown in Fig. 6c, five parallel experiments were conducted for measuring its alcohol percentage by volume using this method. The results (Fig. 6d) showed the calculated alcohol percentages were respectively at 55.12%, 57.36%, 55.27%, 54.83% and 55.27%, respectively. The average alcohol content is 55.66%, with a variance of 0.8969%. The results are in a good agreement with the labled alcohol content, suggesting within a certain range of alcohol concentration, this fluorescent probe has high accuracy and can be used for analyzing and determining the alcohol content in alcoholic beverages.

In recent years, great achivements have been made in the application of fluorescent CDs in various fields, such as information anti-counterfeiting, biological imaging, ion detection, etc. Herein, serving as anti-counterfeiting ink is taken as another example to briefly introduce the application of G-CDs. Diluted G-CDs solution can be used as fluorescent ink for anti-counterfeiting purpose, suitable for use with fountain pens, seals, and inkjet printers to write or print on white paper. However, most fluorescent inks are water-soluble, and their fluorescence intensity will be quenched in water. Therefore, we explored the waterproof performance of G-CDs synthesized in this work. As shown in Fig. 7 , a handwritting word ‘G-Dots’ is written by G-CDs. For comparison. Another word ‘B-CDs’ is written by Blue CDs (B-CDs), which are famous CDs with excellent luminescent properties prepared by citric acid and ethylenediamine. One can see that both the two words are invisible under daylight (Fig. 7a and b) while visible under ultraviolet lamp (Fig. 7c and d). However, after rinsing with tap water, the word ‘G-Dots’ is still visiblewhile the word ‘B-Dots’ disappears completely (Fig. 7e and f). The result indicates that G-CDs used as anti-counterfeiting fluorescent ink have the advantage of good waterproof property over water-soluble fluorescent CDs.

Using cordyceps militaris as the carbon source, we have successfully prepared green CDs (G-CDs) with excellent optical properties and low cost through a solvothermal method. The surface of the G-CDs contains a large number of nitrogen and oxygen functional groups. The excitation and emission wavelengths of G-CDs is 450 nm and 537 nm, respectively. The excitation wavelenth independent emissions suggest that the G-CDs is molecular state CDs, which have unique luminescent center. At the same time, G-CDs exhibit good photobleaching resistance, salt resistance, chemical stability, and relatively high quantum yield (12.9%). The G-CDs can be used as fluorecent probe for alcohol content analysis in alcoholic beverages and information a fluorescent anti-counterfeiting ink for fountain pens, seals and inkjet printers. This work converts low-value biomass materials into high-value carbon nanomaterials with excellent photoemission characteristics, fluorescence stability and low toxicity, further expanding the application range of CDs synthesized from natural biomass materials.

Jing Hu: Investigation, Synthetic Chemistry, Spectral acquisition, Funding acquisition, Writing – original draft. Hongyu Zhou: Investigation, Synthetic Chemistry, Spectral acquisition, Funding acquisition, Writing – original draft. Yanshun Ma: Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Investigation. Shaogui Wu: Supervision, Investigation, computation, Writing – review & editing. Liang Hao: Conceptualization, Discussion, Review.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Thank Prof. Dongmao Zhang, Ph.D Joanna Xiuzhu Xu, Xue Leng et al. in Department of Chemistry, Mississippi State University for helpful advices.
